text: As new academic journals emerge and submission rates climb, editors in political science have become increasingly concerned about "reviewer fatigue." Many assume that an overwhelming volume of requests has made scholars less willing to perform the anonymous, time-consuming service of manuscript evaluation. However, there has been little systematic research into why scholars actually decline these invitations. Using data from the *American Political Science Review*, we empirically investigated the rates at which scholars accept or decline requests, alongside their stated reasons for doing so. Our findings suggest that reviewer fatigue is only one of many factors influencing these decisions. The evidence indicates that while scholars remain generally willing to contribute to the peer-review process, they are often constrained by the competing demands of their professional and personal lives.
